title: How Mountains Of Worm Cocoons Are Turned Into Expensive Silk In Vietnam | Big Business
desc:
Silk craft villages have dotted Vietnam for hundreds of years. They make silk by hand in a tedious 30-step process. Craftspeople feed the silkworms every four hours so they spin brilliantly yellow cocoons. And turning those cocoons into silk is even more delicate work. 

In the 2010s, as cheaper, fake silk flooded the market and young textile workers moved to the big cities, this local craft started dying out. But one village turned things around, banding together into one cooperative called HanhSilk.

We head to northern Vietnam to learn how Nam Cao village saved its big business of silk.

title: Tussar Silk, The Story Of No-Violence Silk From India
desc:
Tussar Silk is produced mainly in the eastern regions of India including Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and West Bengal.

It is also called ‘Wild Silk’. And there is a specific reason for it. These silkworms (Antheraea) are not domesticated creatures. They feed on wild trees instead of being confined to a little farm or an overly controlled environment. 

There are two types of Tussar silk. Pure Tussar and Tussar Ghica. Pure Tussar is made using undamaged cocoons, which means the larvae die inside. On the other hand, Tussar Ghicha is the one in which the moth makes a hole at the top of the cocoon and breaks out and therefore unharmed. The moth flies out, mates, and produces more eggs; more importantly, it’s free!

This is why Tussar Silk has earned the name “No-Violence-Silk”. This is also why there has been a renewed interest in it by designers and textile enthusiasts as it offers ethical practices in fashion and it’s environmentally friendly. Even the trees on which the silkworms feed are not damaged or cut down. It is to be noted that most of the villagers don’t have the concept of violence or non-violence silk. For them, it is a modern fancy term. They have been making Tussar Ghicha silk for centuries.

title: How silk cloth is made | sericulture | life cycle of silkworm | silkworm farm
desc:
How silk cloth is made | sericulture | life cycle of silkworm | silkworm farm

The life cycle of a silkworm, also known as Bombyx mori, consists of four stages:

Stage 1: Egg
- Female silkworms lay eggs on mulberry leaves.
- Eggs hatch into larvae within 3-4 days.

Stage 2: Larva (Caterpillar)
- Larvae feed on mulberry leaves and molt four times.
- This stage lasts around 20-30 days.

Stage 3: Pupa (Cocoon)
- When fully grown, larvae spin a protective cocoon around themselves.
- Inside the cocoon, the larva undergoes metamorphosis, transforming into a pupa.
- This stage lasts around 7-10 days.

Stage 4: Adult (Moth)
- The adult moth emerges from the cocoon.
- Moths mate and lay eggs to start the cycle again.
- Adult moths live for around 5-7 days.

Silkworms are cultivated for their silk, which is harvested by soaking the cocoons in hot water and unwinding the silk fibers.

title: Silkworm making Cocoon | Bombyx Mori | Sericulture
desc:
The cocoon construction by a silkworm involves several stages, typically over two to three days:

1.Spinning Silk : Once the silkworm reaches the mature larval stage, it secretes a fluid silk protein from its silk glands. This fluid hardens when exposed to air, forming silk threads.

2. Forming the Cocoon : The silkworm attaches these silk threads to a surface and begins spinning a continuous thread around itself. It uses figure-eight movements of its head to form the cocoon, which is made of a single, long, unbroken silk fiber.

3. Complete Cocoon : After about two to three days, the silkworm completes the construction, fully enclosing itself inside the cocoon. This cocoon serves as a protective covering as the silkworm undergoes metamorphosis, transforming into a moth. 

Once the process is complete, the cocoon provides a source of raw silk, which is harvested in silk production.

title: Millions Earned from Silkworms in China 🤯
desc:
Millions Earned from Silkworms in China 🤯

#shorts 

Did you know?
In China, people are earning millions of dollars by farming silkworms!
They raise thousands — even millions — of silkworms on their farms.
Every day, the worms are fed fresh mulberry leaves, which help them grow faster.
Once they’re fully grown, they are placed in special nets, where they spin cocoons in just a few days.
These cocoons are then collected and boiled in hot water to extract fine silk threads.
The silk is then spun and woven —
and transformed into luxurious, soft, and valuable quilts.
These quilts are sold in international markets for millions of dollars!

title: Silkworm breeding reaps profits for farmers in southwest China
desc:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here: 
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

The cultivation of silkworm to produce silk, or sericulture, has become a lucrative industry in the city of Bijie in China’s southwest Guizhou province. About 40 people work at a sericulture cooperative, each earning a monthly salary of up to 4,000 yuan (US$593). In efforts to develop the region's silkworm breeding industry, 120 hectares (297 acres) of land have been set aside by the government for growing mulberry trees, the only source of food for silkworms.

title: The mystery of silk: a brief global history of the remarkable material
desc:
From the cocoon of the bombyx mori silk moth, to shiny couture dresses, join us on a journey through the fascinating world of silk – all the way from fibre to thread.

Used in anything from fashion and furnishings, to fishing lines and parachutes, silk is a remarkably versatile material. But how does it stay cool in hot weather, and warm in cold weather? How is silk made? What makes it a strong, yet lightweight fibre? How can it be used to create so many different styles and textures?

title: How Silk is Made: Traditional Silk Production Process from Silkworm to Fabric
desc:
Discover the fascinating journey of silk production, from feeding silkworms to creating luxurious silk fabric. This video explains the traditional process of cultivating silk on agricultural land. Farmers grow the silkworms' favorite green leaves, which are fed to the silkworms, making them big and fat. Watch as the silkworms are carefully collected, spread on round mats, and begin spinning their silk cocoons. Learn how the delicate silk threads are harvested by hand, dried in the sun, boiled in hot water, and finally spun into fine silk fabric on handloom machines. This ancient art of silk-making results in the most expensive and luxurious fabric in the world.

desc:
This time I will bring you a fun loop form! Different from the previous multi-part ensemble, it is possible to complete a piece of music in one go, and the process is quite interesting~

The use of guitar in Hotel California can be regarded as one of the classic arrangements in history, especially the 1994 live version. So I made some deletions in the vocal part, focusing on the two guitar solos of the prelude and the ending. Hope you like it!

The costume this time is Wei Jin style wide-sleeved cross-collared Hanfu. For the convenience of plucking the instrument, panbo are added. desc:

#李子柒#liziqi#lýtửthất#ChineseCuisine#ChineseFood

Papermaking can be traced back to the early Western Han Dynasty in 2nd century BC, when our ancestors began to produce the most primitive form of paper from hemp netting and bark. 
In this highly-industrialized age of mass production, I hope we still have idea how our Chinese ancestors, without any modern machines, invented paper!
手工造纸的起源最早可追溯到公元前2世纪时的西汉初年，那时我们的祖先便开始用麻皮和树皮制成最原始的纸张。在这个已经拥有了大量机械化生产纸张的年代，但愿我们还能知晓祖先们是怎样用一双手，让中国成为了世界上最早发明纸的国家！

title: Another year, another reunion dinner. Have you gone home for the New Year?【滇西小哥】
desc:
※Click "cc" on the lower right menu to choose your subtitle language.

This year, my aunt said she wanted to cook with me, so our family started a "one person, one dish" New Year's Eve dinner. Everyone made their favourite signature dish... With so many dishes, we decided to arrange them into a Peacock Feast. In Yunnan, people often use peacocks to symbolize beauty and good things. The Peacock Feast carries the meaning of having a beautifully prepared meal for the New Year.

title: Traditional Chinese Handmade Craft | Making a Jade Pen Shaft Calligraphy Brush by Hand #handmade
desc:
The calligraphy brush is an essential tool in traditional Chinese calligraphy and a beautiful craft.  
The artisan first cuts and polishes jade into a cylindrical shape, then carves it into a comfortable and aesthetically pleasing pen shaft.  
Next, the brush tip is made from high-quality hairs, carefully washed, selected, trimmed, and bound through multiple steps.  
The brush hairs are precisely attached to the pen shaft, ensuring smooth writing with proper elasticity.  
The entire process is entirely handmade, with each brush representing traditional skill, patience, and a vital part of Chinese cultural heritage.

title: Ancient Chinese Movable Type Printing | Cultural Heritage, Education & Tourism Documentary
desc:
Ancient Chinese Movable Type Printing | Cultural Heritage, Education & Tourism Documentary

📝 Description:
Discover the timeless art of Movable Type Printing, one of the most influential ancient Chinese inventions that shaped the history of knowledge and communication.

In this educational and cultural documentary, we explore the beauty of traditional craftsmanship, the wisdom of history, and the role of heritage preservation in modern society. From ancient printing blocks to the influence on today’s education, creative industries, and cultural tourism, this film highlights how tradition continues to inspire the future.

Perfect for students, educators, cultural travelers, and history enthusiasts, this documentary connects the artistry of the past with the innovation of the present. Learn how heritage contributes to luxury art, sustainable culture, and lifelong learning.

✨ Subscribe now for more inspiring stories from China’s intangible cultural heritage and world history.

title: Dragon Boat Festival Feast: Use natural containers like leaves and bamboo to create delicious dishes
desc:
※Click "cc" on the lower right menu to choose your subtitle language.

In Yunnan cuisine, there is a category of dishes that need to be wrapped in natural containers. Zongzi leaves and bamboo shoot husks are used to wrap zongzi, banana leaves are used for roasted dishes, lotus leaves can be used for lotus leaf chicken and sticky rice, and bamboo tubes can be used to steam and roast all kinds of food. The amazing thing is that whatever is used to wrap the food imparts its flavor to it, enhancing the richness and deliciousness of the dishes!

The day we had our meal, we were really lucky because there were flying ants everywhere. Actually, our family had postponed the meal for two days, but it was quite unfortunate that during the Dragon Boat Festival, there were flying ants every day. These insects are attracted to light and are quite common in the village during this season. Usually, when there are flying ants at home, we turn off all the lights. However, for filming, we couldn’t do that. I included a text warning in the video where the flying ants appear, so viewers can watch with caution. For us, flying ants are a good omen, indicating a bountiful harvest of wild mushrooms and plentiful rain. They are also edible insects. My family seemed quite happy about it. We're used to seeing them, so we don't find it uncomfortable. I also advised viewers in the video to skip this part if they don’t like it.

Finally, I wish everyone happiness and safety every day!

Hello, everyone! I am Dianxi Xiaoge, a Yunnan native. If you enjoy my videos, please keep subscribing to my channel. I'll continue to share my life and Yunnan-styled food in my uploads. Wish you have a happy life and enjoy tasty food every day

desc:
Learn Tai Chi from the beginning with *Dr Paul Lam* . This is the free first lesson from the *Tai Chi for Beginners* program, including introduction, how to use the program, staying safe, warm-up exercises, your first movements, and cool-down.

*What is Tai Chi for Beginners?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is Dr Paul Lam's program suitable for beginners, consisting of fluid, gentle and relaxed movements suitable for anyone and can be practised almost anywhere. It includes the Six Easy Steps, including four Qigong exercises, five foundational movements (including Tai Chi Walking), and the Tai Chi for Beginners set based on the 24 Forms, the world's most popular tai chi set. Starting with warm-up exercises, Dr Lam guides you steadily through each step as though you were in his class. This program improves muscular strength, flexibility, balance, and confidence.

*What will I learn in this free lesson?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is suitable for all ages and fitness levels. You can practise seated or standing, and adjust the depth of your movements to what feels comfortable.

title: 帝女花之香夭 Fragrant Sacrifice 任白名曲Yam Kim Fei and Bak Suit Sin
desc:
This is probably the most well-known excerpt from the most-watched Cantonese Opera in the world.  I have supplied English subtitles here, hoping that those who are not familiar with this language can enjoy it too.

I have uploaded another version before, performed by the students of these two actresses.

帝女花 is a Cantonese Opera first made into a movie in 1959, starring Opera singers, Yam Kim Fei (任剑辉) and Bak Suit Sin (白雪仙), both ladies, but Yam impersonated a man in all her movies. 

The Emperor of the Ming dynasty had just approved of his future son-in-law who won the heart of his daughter with poetry. At that moment, The Ching army attacked and took over the palace. The Emperor committed suicide with his family but Princess Cheung Ping survived. To win the favour of his subjects, the new Emperor of the Ching dynasty adopted the Princess and proceeded with the grand wedding. To show their loyalty to the Ming emperor, the newly-weds take their own lives on their wedding night. The Cantonese Opera is too beautiful to be translated. Something will be "lost in translation." The Song here, 香夭 (Fragrant Sacrifice) is the grand finale of the opera, sung on the wedding night, the couple are dressed in exquisite finery. The Princess and her groom express their love for each other and their loyalty to the former dynasty, especially the late Emperor, before they take their own lives, drinking wine poisoned with Arsenic. 

I have added English subtitles for my friends here on youtube. Hope you will like this as much as I do. Regards, Jeremy

title: Traditional Chinese Paper Trimming with a Half-Moon Knife
desc:
A Little Wisdom Helps You Become Smarter !

Cutting a Stack of Xuan Paper with a Curved Hand Blade

In the video, a skilled artisan is using a traditional half-moon-shaped paper knife to trim a large stack of Xuan paper. The paper is tightly pressed under a simple wooden frame to keep it aligned and secure. With precise and steady hands, the worker pulls the sharp curved blade along the edge of the wooden guide, cutting smoothly through the entire stack in one motion. This traditional method is used in Chinese papermaking and bookbinding workshops to neatly trim the rough edges of handmade paper. The curved knife allows long, continuous cuts without damaging the delicate paper fibers. This technique ensures every sheet is uniformly sized with clean edges, ready for calligraphy, painting, or printing. The process highlights the efficiency and elegance of handmade paper finishing. Using simple tools, experienced crafters can achieve results that are difficult to replicate even with modern machines. This video offers a glimpse into a time-honored craft that values precision, patience, and practicality. Viewers can appreciate the seamless blend of tool, material, and human skill in this delicate yet decisive act of cutting.

title: Beating blue sandalwood bark: Extracting fibers for handmade Xuan papermaking
desc: The bark of the blue sandalwood tree, also known as the blue sandalwood, is a natural material rich in fibers. The bark is tough and elastic, commonly used in traditional handmade papermaking. During the papermaking process, the bark is beaten and soaked to extract fibers, which are then used to make high-quality Xuan paper. The fibers from the blue sandalwood bark are fine in texture, helping the paper maintain good strength and flexibility, while also offering excellent water absorption and breathability. It is an essential material in Chinese traditional culture.

title: The Hard Part of Inventing The Printing Press
desc:
The invention of the printing press in the 15th century revolutionized the world by enabling mass production of written material. Its impact on communication, education, and the dissemination of knowledge was profound. However, the development of this groundbreaking technology was not without its challenges.

One of the major hurdles was the creation of movable type, a system where individual characters could be arranged and rearranged to form words and sentences. Metal typefaces had to be crafted with precision and uniformity, allowing for efficient printing. This required the mastery of metalworking techniques and the creation of durable alloys that could withstand the pressure of the printing process.

In addition to the technological aspects, chemical advancements were essential. The invention of oil-based inks with the right viscosity and drying properties was crucial. Printers had to experiment with various pigments, binders, and drying agents to achieve optimal results.  Another hurdle was developing the. right metal to be used for the typeblocks. The metal needed to be soft enough to be easily melted and molted, but durable enough to withstand the pressures of the printing process.

Overcoming these challenges required the ingenuity and collaboration of inventors, craftsmen, chemists, and scholars.

Hello! 

Recently on Reddit, I posted a poll about which video I should make: https://www.reddit.com/r/Hordecore/
Taking the votes into account, also interests of my friends who wanted to learn Mongolian throat singing, I present you "how to throat sing in 3 easy steps!" =)

It might take some dedication to get it better, it could be a bit challenging for girls but personally, I've seen a live show of a Tuvan woman, musician, and artist Sainkho, who can do a few forms of this culture. Another person was a female student I met a few years ago. She used to learn how to play morin khuur with a few boys when she was young, the boys were learning khoomei. They teased her that she can't sing it because she is a girl, so she got irritated and practiced morin khuur with khoomei at home. After some time, it seems she was able to sing as well as any other person. There is another woman who can sing it as well, she plays in Tuvan folk-rock band Yat-Kha :)

In any case, let me know in the comments below if you have questions or ideas. Oh, one detail I forgot to mention in the video is that this type of singing is specifically called "kargyraa". The other one is called "isgeree"(or sygyt in Tuvan) is quite difficult to learn, I still haven't mastered it fully. The intro music was done originally by ElectroKaplosion - coffin dance music with Mongolian theme, a super cool dude, check out his channel :D - https://www.youtube.com/user/ElectroKaplosion

title: Mazu e (Abbé Karol Abessolo) — Le Chœur des Piccoli (Version acoustique) [paroles + traduction]
desc:
Mazu e est un chant camerounais de l'abbe Karol Mario Abessolo (de regrettée mémoire) en langue ewondo et repris ici en version acoustique par le Choeur des Piccoli. Au delà de la musique c'est une vraie prière chantée. 

Ces paroles profondes écrites par l'abbé Karol Mario Abessolo, ont rendu la chanson célèbre dans le répertoire du chant choral en langue beti/ewondo. Cette interprétation, à laquelle nous avons ajouté les paroles et la traduction, privilégie la chaleur des voix et l’intimité du message. Le Chant Mazu e Il est utilisé dans les liturgies catholiques camerounaises surtout comme chant d'entrée, et est repris non seulement par les chorales Ewondo mais aussi les chorales dans d'autres langues camerounaises.

==============
Compositeur : Karol Mario Abessolo.
Lead: Raphaël (Le Choeur des Piccoli)
Choeurs: Anne Sophie, Roxane, Raphaël (Le Choeur des Piccoli)
Piano: Alexis BAKOND
Mix et Master: Kisito Sound Studio
Langue : beti / ewondo (utilisée au Cameroun). 
Transcription des paroles et traduction: Georges Eric Mbah

===============
Paroles:

Refrain: Mazu e, mazu ée a Nti mazu, mezu dzali nyian(e) woé, mazu éé, a Nti, Mazu éé, mazu ée a Nti mazu, mezu dzali nyian(e) woé, mazu éé, a Nti mazu.

1. A mindzug miama menga yanga Nti o me loege nye o a Nti, (mazu). Ndo Nti a ke alô abe etuga dzie a wog(e) ntad wom oo , (mazu), anga dudu ma edog ebe o a tele ma mekol a tsa(mazu), owe ma yebe nloé Nti o, owe a Nti me so ya.

2. Ndo Nti asô me kalan mpkaman dzia, menga tag ai nyé oo, (mazu), dzia mefon, dzia’olugu o, asu Zamba wom oo (mazu), eban oyeng olugu ai duma, bibô ai Ntondobe oo, (mazu), owe, matag ai Nti Zamba wam(e), Tara Nti mafon ai wa.

3. A Nti wa ndiki diñ zeze metunga, ai zeze bifae (mazu), ve da’a Nti onga yiè ma melô ne me wog ebug dzoé a (mazu), onga teb minkpel biyem o mena menyô mesô ya (m’azu), owe matuni wa mamen a Nti, Tara Nti m’atuni wa enyiñ dzama a.

4. Ongá dudu ma edog ebe o a Nti Ntondobe oo (m’azu), be bonde bama ai bemvoé bama b'ayem na meke ya a (m’azu). Ebá bësë b'ayén ma a Nti, b'ayén ve awu aa (m’azu), m'etam etam o, okòn wama m'ayem na m'eńiñ aa (m’azu), a Nti, zëzë nsem minsëm o, m'ave wa ntud ngañ aa.

===============
Mazu e a été Enregistré et mixé chez Kisito Sound Studio (Alexis BAKOND). La vidéo Lyrics avec traduction en langue française aussi.

=============

How do you make tea? Chances are you’ve been doing it wrong.

For many people in China, brewing tea is a ritual akin to meditation. And there is a proper ceremony called gongfu cha.

Gongfu means skill, and cha means tea. It’s a form of Chinese tea service that dates back to the 14th century in Fujian. It places emphasis on the tea’s taste, temperature, and quality.

title: 宣纸 Xuan paper #springonshorts
desc:
Xuan paper originates from Jing County, Anhui Province, named after ancient Xuanzhou. It is a precious traditional Chinese painting and calligraphy paper with a thousand-year history. It took shape in the Tang Dynasty, was widely used in painting and calligraphy in the Song and Yuan dynasties, and matured in the Ming and Qing dynasties as a treasure among the Four Treasures of the Study.
Made of wingceltis bark and sandy-field rice straw via over a hundred manual procedures, it features tough texture, even ink absorption, insect resistance and durability, famed as "millennium-lasting paper".
Favored by ancient artists, it bears countless classic artworks. Ideal for calligraphy, painting, ancient book reproduction and seal mounting, its craftsmanship is now listed as intangible cultural heritage, an indispensable gem of oriental ink culture.

title: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
desc:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00425
Description: Acupuncture and moxibustion are forms of traditional Chinese medicine widely practised in China and also found in regions of south-east Asia, Europe and the Americas. The theories of acupuncture and moxibustion hold that the human body acts as a small universe connected by channels, and that by physically stimulating these channels the practitioner can promote the human body's self-regulating functions and bring health to the patient. This stimulation involves the burning of moxa (mugwort) or the insertion of needles into points on these channels, with the aim to restore the body's balance and prevent and treat disease. In acupuncture, needles are selected according to the individual condition and used to puncture and stimulate the chosen points. Moxibustion is usually divided into direct and indirect moxibustion, in which either moxa cones are placed directly on points or moxa sticks are held and kept at some distance from the body surface to warm the chosen area. Moxa cones and sticks are made of dried mugwort leaves. Acupuncture and moxibustion are taught through verbal instruction and demonstration, transmitted through master-disciple relations or through members of a clan. Currently, acupuncture and moxibustion are also transmitted through formal academic education.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 Institute of Acupuncture and Moxibustion

title: Peking opera
desc:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00418
Description: Peking opera is a performance art incorporating singing, reciting, acting, martial arts. Although widely practised throughout China, its performance centres on Beijing, Tianjin and Shanghai. Peking opera is sung and recited using primarily Beijing dialect, and its librettos are composed according to a strict set of rules that prize form and rhyme. They tell stories of history, politics, society and daily life and aspire to inform as they entertain. The music of Peking opera plays a key role in setting the pace of the show, creating a particular atmosphere, shaping the characters, and guiding the progress of the stories. 'Civilian plays' emphasize string and wind instruments such as the thin, high-pitched ''jinghu ''and the flute ''dizi, ''while 'military plays' feature percussion instruments like the ''bangu ''or ''daluo. ''Performance is characterized by a formulaic and symbolic style with actors and actresses following established choreography for movements of hands, eyes, torsos, and feet. Traditionally, stage settings and props are kept to a minimum. Costumes are flamboyant and the exaggerated facial make-up uses concise symbols, colours and patterns to portray characters' personalities and social identities. Peking opera is transmitted largely through master-student training with trainees learning basic skills through oral instruction, observation and imitation. It is regarded as an expression of the aesthetic ideal of opera in traditional Chinese society and remains a widely recognized element of the country's cultural heritage.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 by Han wen / Beijing Bureau of Culture

title: Peking Opera 《execute ChenShimei》 Tom and Jerry meme Provenance  Ching Chen Hanji  京剧《铡美案》
desc:
During the Northern Song Dynasty, Chen Shimei entered the capital to take the exam, and was the number one champion, and was selected as emperor's son-in-law. His hometown has been drought year after year, his parents died, his wife Qin Xianglian and her children went to Beijing to find her husband, but broke into the palace and was expelled. Prime Minister Wang Yanling sympathized with her and tried to make Qin Xianglian play at the banquet dressed as a singer on Chen Shimei's birthday to help them reconcile, but failed. Wang Yanling sent Qin Xianglian a fan as a token and told her to go to Kaifeng Mansion to find Bao Zheng to sue (Bao Zheng was a famous, powerful and upright official in ancient China). Chen Shimei sent a private arm to Han Qi to hunt down. Han Qi let Qin Xianglian and her son go because he sympathized with them, but committed suicide because he failed to complete the task. Qin Xianglian fled and went to Bao Zheng's official office to accuse Chen Shimei of "killing his wife and child." Bao Zheng summoned Chen Shimei to Kaifeng Mansion to persuade them to reconcile, but Chen Shimei did not cooperate. Bao Zheng asked Qin Xianglian and Chen Shimei to confront each other. Chen Shimei relied on his noble status to argue with strong words; Bao Zheng was angry and prepared to execute Chen Shimei. The princess and queen mother rushed to stop the execution. In order to maintain the justice of the law, Bao Zheng continued to execute his sentence and executed Chen Shimei.
This video tells one of the stories above, what happened after Qin Xianglian arrived at Bao Zheng’s official office
